summary: 13th-century Dominican friar and Scholastic philosopher, author of Summa Theologica and Summa Contra Gentiles. Known for the Five Ways (proofs of God's existence), the doctrine of double effect, natural law theory, and his synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology. Student of Albertus Magnus, called 'The Philosopher' by his followers referring to Aristotle and 'The Commentator' referring to Averroes.

works.name: The Five Ways (Quinque Viae)
works.description: Thomas Aquinas's five proofs of God's existence from the Summa Theologica are among the most frequently clued philosophical arguments in quizbowl. They include the argument from motion (unmoved mover), first cause, contingency, degree (there must be an ultimate goodness), and teleology. Notably, they do not include the ontological argument. Frederick Copleston divided them into in fieri and in esse classes.

clues.clue: Five proofs/ways of God's existence in Summa Theologica (quinque viae)
examples: This philosopher reintroduced the cosmological 'first mover' argument as one of the five proofs of the existence of God
name this author of the Summa Theologica

clues.clue: First way: unmoved mover / argument from motion - all movement must be caused by something that does not change
examples: The first of these statements claims that something must cause change without itself changing, called the concept of the 'unmoved mover.'
presented a theory of mind along with five proofs of God's existence in Summa Theologica

clues.clue: Fourth way: argument from degree - since things have properties to varying degrees, something must have them maximally (God)
examples: Because all objects have properties, and some entity has properties to the maximum degree, this thinker concluded that God must therefore exist.
the analogy of locating a 'worm lower down' and a 'dog higher' is used in one of these statements

clues.clue: The Five Ways notably do NOT include the ontological argument
examples: They notably do not include the ontological argument.

clues.clue: Frederick Copleston divided them into in fieri and in esse classes
examples: Frederick Copleston divided these statements into in fieri and in esse classes.

clues.clue: Richard Swinburne's 1979 book criticized emphasis on 'change' rather than 'existence' in one proof
examples: The emphasis on 'change,' rather than 'existence' in one of these statements was criticized in a 1979 book by Richard Swinburne.

works.name: Doctrine of Double Effect
works.description: Aquinas formulated the principle that an action with harmful but unintended side effects may be morally permissible if the action itself is good, the intention is good, and the good results outweigh the bad. This became enormously influential: Philippa Foot's trolley problem paper popularized it, and G.E.M. Anscombe, H.L.A. Hart, and others engaged with it extensively.

clues.clue: Formulated doctrine of double effect: actions with harmful but unintended side effects may be permissible
examples: This philosopher wrote that intended harm should not be caused to create a positive outcome in his doctrine of double effect.
This thinker theorized that behaviors with harmful outcomes might be just in certain circumstances with his 'Principle of Double Effect.'

clues.clue: Philippa Foot's trolley problem paper popularized the doctrine; her paper titled 'The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of the Double Effect'
examples: Philippa Foot's trolley problem popularized this thinker's principle that an action with unforeseen negative consequences may still be permissible
That principle first discussed by this philosopher appears alongside 'abortion' in the title of Philippa Foot's paper originating the trolley problem.

clues.clue: G.E.M. Anscombe discussed a misapplication involving drug administration and a forcibly moved fat man
examples: According to Anscombe, a concept first introduced by this male thinker was misapplied in an example concerning drug administration that appears in a paper which also considers forcibly moving a fat man.

clues.clue: H.L.A. Hart gave the doctrine 'short shrift' in Punishment and Responsibility
examples: a concept first formulated by this philosopher was said to have been 'given short shrift' by H.L.A. Hart in Punishment and Responsibility

clues.clue: 'nothing hinders one act from having two effects' - argued harm can occur 'beside the intention'
examples: this philosopher suggested that one might cause events 'beside the intention' after saying that 'nothing hinders one act from having two effects.'

works.name: Natural Law and the Treatise on Law
works.description: Aquinas revived natural law theory by distinguishing four kinds of law: eternal, natural, human, and divine. He defined law as 'an ordinance of reason for the common good' and argued that the first precept of natural law is that 'good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided.' His just war criteria (just purpose, proper authority, right intention) built on Augustine, and the Salamanca School later expanded his just price concept.

clues.clue: Distinguished four kinds of law: eternal, natural, human, divine
examples: This philosopher distinguished four kinds of law: eternal, natural, human and divine.
He separated out eternal, divine, natural, and human forms of law, of which eternal law consists of the mind of God.

clues.clue: Defined law as 'an ordinance of reason for the common good' (Adrian Vermeule's common-good constitutionalism draws on this)
examples: Adrian Vermeule's common-good constitutionalism takes its name from this thinker's definition of law as 'an ordinance of reason for the common good.'

clues.clue: John Finnis argued Aquinas derived all norms from the self-evident first precept: 'good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided'
examples: John Finnis argues that he derived all norms from the self-evident 'first precept' that 'good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided.'

clues.clue: Just war criteria: just purpose, proper authority, right intention (building on Augustine)
examples: This philosopher listed just purpose, proper authority, and right intention as criteria for a just war
One section of his major work lays out the criteria for 'just war.'

clues.clue: Treatise on Law compares 'Old Law' and 'New Law'; legislators choose through determinatio
examples: A treatise by this philosopher which extensively compares the 'Old Law' and the 'New Law' describes how lawmakers decide between different options through a process called determinatio.

clues.clue: Compared legislator's job to a craftsman building a house - freedom in shape as long as it serves purpose
examples: This philosopher compared the job of a legislator to that of a craftsman who has freedom to decide what shape to build a house in, as long as it serves its specified purpose.

clues.clue: Condemned usury, comparing it to charging for both a cake and the eating of the cake
examples: This thinker compared usury to charging for both a cake and for the eating of the cake.

works.name: Summa Theologica (Structure and Reception)
works.description: Aquinas's magnum opus, divided into three parts and 512 questions (3,125 articles answering 614 questions in some counts). Written in a question-and-answer format where objections are answered with 'sed contra' ('on the contrary'). It refers to Aristotle as 'The Philosopher' and Averroes as 'The Commentator.' Leo XIII's encyclical Aeterni Patris revived interest in Thomism, and analytical Thomism was later developed by John Haldane.

clues.clue: Magnum opus with 'sed contra' response format; refers to Aristotle as 'The Philosopher' and Averroes as 'The Commentator'
examples: That work, which refers to Averroes as 'The Commentator' and Aristotle as 'The Philosopher,' presents five arguments for God's existence.
This philosopher's longest work is written in a question and answer format in which answers are presented with the response 'sed contra'

clues.clue: Leo XIII's encyclical Aeterni Patris revived Thomist studies
examples: The encyclical Aeterni Patris was delivered in support of this thinker
the study of whose work was espoused by Leo XIII's bull Aeterni Patris

clues.clue: John Haldane developed analytical Thomism blending Aquinas with analytic philosophy
examples: John Haldane belongs to a school that blends this philosopher with analytic philosophy.
The Cracow Circle applied formal logic to the ideas of this thinker, whom John Haldane synthesized with analytical philosophy.

clues.clue: Etienne Tempier condemned 'errors' of Thomism in 1277 Condemnations
examples: Etienne Tempier condemned 'errors' of this philosophy as part of 1277 Condemnations.

clues.clue: G.K. Chesterton called him a 'Dumb Ox' in a book about him
examples: Chesterton called the founder of this school of thought a 'Dumb Ox' in a book about him.

clues.clue: Roberto Busa spent 8 years compiling notecards; partnered with IBM for 56-volume index (first work of digital humanities)
examples: Busa later partnered with IBM to produce a 56-volume index devoted to the writings of this man, often credited as the first work of digital humanities.

clues.clue: Alasdair MacIntyre in After Virtue became a devotee because Aquinas was 'a better Aristotelian than Aristotle'
examples: Alasdair Macintyre claims to have become a devotee of this philosopher because he was 'in some respects a better Aristotelian than Aristotle.'

works.name: Summa Contra Gentiles
works.description: A treatise likely written for missionaries, intended to convince non-Christians of the value of God. It deliberately refrains from citing the Old or New Testament and is split into discussions of human truths and divine truths. May have been written at the behest of Raymond of Penafort.

clues.clue: Written for missionaries; refrains from citing scripture; split into human and divine truths
examples: this philosopher's works includes a book that refrains from citing the Old or New Testament since it was intended as a treatise for missionaries
One of his works may have been written at the behest of Raymond of Penafort and is split into a discussion of human truths and divine truths.

clues.clue: States that 'the senses are cognizant only of singulars' while intellect is 'cognizant of universals'
examples: This thinker wrote that the senses are 'cognizant only of singulars' while the intellect is 'cognizant of universals' in a treatise likely meant to aid missionaries.

works.name: Analogy of Being and Metaphysics
works.description: Aquinas proposed the Analogia Entis ('Analogy of Being'), arguing that God's similarity with created things can only be affirmed while also affirming dissimilarity - properties like 'goodness' are predicated analogically, not univocally (contra Duns Scotus). He also argued that essence and existence are distinct in all beings except God, building on Avicenna. His quiddity concept is often contrasted with Scotus's haecceity.

clues.clue: Analogia Entis / Analogy of Being: God's goodness is only analogous to human goodness (analogical predication, not univocal)
examples: He held that human goodness was only akin to God's goodness, arguing that predication is analogical.
This theologian proposed the idea that God's similarity with created things can only be affirmed while also affirming God's dissimilarity, a concept called the Analogia Entis

clues.clue: Karl Barth called Analogia Entis 'the antichrist' after reading Erich Przywara's book on it
examples: A book by Erich Przywara titled for one of this thinker's ideas prompted Karl Barth to call that idea 'the antichrist.'

clues.clue: Used example of healthy urine vs. healthy bodies to distinguish forms of analogical predication
examples: He used the example of healthy urine vs. healthy bodies to distinguish between forms of 'analogical predication.'

clues.clue: Essence and existence are distinct in all beings except God (building on Avicenna); 'a substance is a composite of essence and existence'
examples: Building on Avicenna, this philosopher claimed that essence is dependent on but not synonymous with existence, except in the case of God.
Thomas Aquinas stated that a substance is a composite of these two concepts, since both are required for something to be.

clues.clue: His quiddity ('whatness') contrasted with Duns Scotus's haecceity ('thisness')
examples: Duns Scotus's concept of haecceity is often contrasted with this philosopher's quiddity or 'whatness.'

works.name: Eucharistic Hymns
works.description: Pope Urban IV commissioned Aquinas to write five hymns for the feast of Corpus Christi, including 'Adoro te devote,' 'Pange lingua gloriosi corpus mysterium,' and 'O Salutaris Hostia.' Josquin des Prez's last mass was based on Pange lingua. Gerard Manley Hopkins rendered one line as 'Lend this life to me then: feed and feast my mind.'

clues.clue: Pope Urban IV commissioned five hymns for Corpus Christi including 'Adoro te devote' and 'Pange lingua'
examples: Pope Urban IV commissioned this author to write for the feast of Corpus Christi
The last mass by Josquin was based on a hymn by this author that begins, 'Tell, tongue, the mystery of the glorious Body.'

clues.clue: Josquin des Prez's last mass (Missa Pange Lingua) was based on Aquinas's Pange lingua hymn
examples: Josquin's Missa Pange Lingua was based on the hymn 'Pange lingua gloriosi corpus mysterium' by this hymnodist

clues.clue: Adoro te devote addresses Jesus as 'Pious Pelican' and includes 'Behold, the bread of angels'
examples: Another hymn by this author prays, 'clean me, the unclean, with Your Blood' in a stanza that addresses Jesus as the 'Pious Pelican.'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Common identifiers: Dominican friar, student of Albertus Magnus (who defended him against the 'dumb ox' reputation), Doctor Angelicus. Studied at the University of Paris where he disputed with Siger de Brabant over Averroism. Argued against the 'unity of the intellect' proposed by Averroes. Remarked that all his work seemed like 'straw' and refused to write for the last four years of his life. Originated the Peripatetic Axiom: 'nothing is in the intellect that was not first in the senses.'

A book central to this philosophical school posited 'Nothing is in the intellect that was not first in the senses' as its Peripatetic axiom

clues.clue: Disputed with Siger de Brabant at University of Paris over Averroism; argued against 'unity of the intellect'

clues.clue: Argued the soul is 'the form of the body' (hylomorphic dualism) in commentary on De Anima; rejected plurality of substantial forms
examples: This hylomorphic dualist claimed that the soul is simply 'the form of the body' in a commentary on Aristotle's De anima.
This philosopher argued that, since the soul is immaterial, humans would not exist as unities if there were a plurality of substantial forms.

clues.clue: Conception of beauty: integritas, consonantia, and claritas
examples: This philosopher's conception of beauty involved three qualities, termed integritas, consonantia, and claritas.

clues.clue: Argued eyes are colorless so they may understand colors; mind is immaterial to understand materiality

clues.clue: Jacques Maritain said Aquinas's thought is 'not a museum piece'
examples: Thought influenced by this man is 'not a museum piece,' according to Jacques Maritain.

comprehensive_summary:
Thomas Aquinas is a 13th-century Dominican friar and the preeminent Scholastic philosopher, most famous for his Summa Theologica. He was a student of Albertus Magnus, who defended him against his early reputation as a 'dumb ox.' He studied at the University of Paris, where he disputed with Siger de Brabant over Averroism and argued against the 'unity of the intellect' proposed by Averroes.

The Five Ways (quinque viae) from the Summa Theologica are Aquinas's five proofs of God's existence: the argument from motion (unmoved mover), first cause, contingency, degree (there must be an ultimate perfection), and teleology. Notably, they do not include the ontological argument. Frederick Copleston divided them into in fieri and in esse classes.

Aquinas formulated the doctrine of double effect, arguing that an action with harmful but unintended side effects may be permissible if the action and intention are good and the good outweighs the bad. He wrote 'nothing hinders one act from having two effects.' Philippa Foot's trolley problem paper popularized this principle, and G.E.M. Anscombe, H.L.A. Hart, and contemporary experimental philosophers like Joshua Knobe have engaged with it.

In natural law theory, Aquinas distinguished four kinds of law (eternal, natural, human, divine) and defined law as 'an ordinance of reason for the common good.' John Finnis argued he derived all norms from the first precept: 'good is to be done and pursued, and evil is to be avoided.' Aquinas also set out criteria for just war (just purpose, proper authority, right intention) building on Augustine.

The Summa Theologica's structure uses a question-and-answer format with 'sed contra' responses. It refers to Aristotle as 'The Philosopher' and Averroes as 'The Commentator.' Leo XIII's encyclical Aeterni Patris revived Thomist studies, and John Haldane later developed analytical Thomism. Roberto Busa's IBM partnership to index the Summa is often credited as the first work of digital humanities. The Summa Contra Gentiles was written for missionaries and deliberately avoids scriptural citations.

Aquinas proposed the Analogia Entis ('Analogy of Being'), arguing that God's properties are predicated analogically rather than univocally (contrasting with Duns Scotus's univocity of being). Karl Barth called this idea 'the antichrist.' Aquinas argued that essence and existence are distinct in all beings except God, building on Avicenna.

Additional clued facts include: he originated the Peripatetic Axiom ('nothing is in the intellect that was not first in the senses'), argued the soul is 'the form of the body' while rejecting plurality of substantial forms, defined beauty in terms of integritas, consonantia, and claritas, and wrote five Corpus Christi hymns including 'Pange lingua' (basis for Josquin's last mass) and 'Adoro te devote.' He reportedly remarked that all his work seemed like 'straw' and stopped writing four years before his death.

Philosopher: the fourth of his five ways uses the analogy of a 'worm lower down' and a 'dog higher' to argue that varying degrees of perfection necessitate a maximally perfect being

Philosopher: Philippa Foot's trolley problem paper titled 'The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of the Double Effect' popularized this thinker's principle
Philosopher: distinguished four kinds of law (eternal, natural, human, divine) and defined law as 'an ordinance of reason for the common good'
